OUTPUT 1: THE CUSTOMER EMAIL

Subject: Quick recap from our call

Hi Brad,

Thanks again for making the time today. I appreciated the straight talk and the context on where things stand as you normalize the IT function and set up for the next phase.

From what I understand, Vergent LMS, Inc. has come a long way from the period last fall when the team was stretched thin and you needed extra coverage to catch up on the basics that were starting to create noise with auditors and internally. With Aptum involved, you were able to stabilize, get patching and operational hygiene back under control, and build a steadier day to day rhythm while your internal team got its footing again. [Vergent LM...ation Chat | Event], [office.com]

What is making this harder now is that the environment and the people around it are changing at the same time. Andrew is leaving at the end of the month, a new Andrew is joining full time, and you are also in the middle of removing a meaningful amount of lower environment footprint where round the clock coverage no longer feels justified. Production still needs immediate support when it matters, but lower and dev issues can be handled by your internal team during business hours with the alerting you already have in place, and that shift naturally creates pressure to realign scope and cost so the contract reflects the new reality as endpoints and monitoring come out. [Vergent LM...ation Chat | Event], [Vergent x...stems chat | Event]

The turning point I heard is that you are trying to normalize now, not later, so you can stop paying for a temporary posture and put that spend toward growth. The modernization work is moving forward in parallel, with lower environments transitioning away from traditional virtual machines toward managed web applications, and databases moving off self managed virtual machines toward managed MariaDB services in Azure. That creates a clear moment to step back and ask what level of support you actually need going into the next contract term, especially with the new Andrew taking ownership of the decision once he is in seat and has had time to work with everyone. [Vergent x...stems chat | Event], [Vergent LM...ation Chat | Event], [Vergent -...arch 2026) | Word]

If this plays out the way you described, success looks like a support model and commercial structure that tracks to what is truly mission critical, protects production, and still keeps you confident going into a growth year. In the near term, that means we land the warm handover between the outgoing and incoming Andrew, reconcile the lower environment endpoint count so we are all working from the same baseline, and align on the database migration glidepath through your October target while acknowledging some production databases may remain due to existing customer commitments. From there, we can translate the drawdown into a clean adjustment to scope and billing, so you are not funding yesterday’s footprint while you focus on onboarding new clients and scaling the right parts of the platform. [Vergent LM...ation Chat | Event], [Vergent -...arch 2026) | Word]

If I missed the nuance on which lower environment items still need any light touch support, or how you want us to treat web apps versus virtual machines in the endpoint math, please correct me. I would rather get the story right now so our delivery teams step into the next conversation with the right context.

Talk soon,

Your Name] OUTPUT 2: CONFIDENCE & GAPS ASSESSMENT     CONFIDENCE & GAPS     =================     Email metadata       No gaps identified for customer first name, interaction type, or sender placeholder     Setting:       🟢       → Grounded in the call transcript and existing Vergent context     Complication:  🟢       → Clear drivers: people transition, scope drawdown in lower environments, need to realign service levels and cost     Turning Point: 🟡       → I inferred the core catalyst as “normalize spend now to fund growth” tied to the modernization push and leadership handoff, but I do not have a single explicit sentence that states the primary trigger in those exact words       Questions to validate         What was the biggest reason to revisit this now: budget pressure, contract timing around September, or operational confidence after stabilizing the team         Is the new Andrew expected to keep the same overall relationship but tune service levels, or is he considering a more material change in how support is structured     Resolution:    🟡       → Desired business outcomes are clear, but a few specifics remain ambiguous and should be confirmed before we speak with delivery teams .docx&action=default&mobileredirect=true&DefaultItemOpen=1)       Questions to validate         When you say “production still needs immediate support,” what does “good” look like in practice: on call coverage only for production, or also for a defined subset of shared services         For the databases, you mentioned an October target tied to the DB contract, but also that some will remain due to specific customer contracts. Which systems are in the “must remain” bucket, and what is the best current estimate of how many that is, since we heard a wide range         Commercially, would you prefer a clean mid term adjustment now, or a broader renegotiation that could also extend the end date, since you floated flexibility there If you want, I can also generate a second version of the email that is shorter and more executive in tone while keeping the same four paragraph narrative.

updated prompt:

 

ou are an account manager writing a short customer story to send back to the customer for confirmation.

Your goal is NOT to summarize meeting notes.

Your goal is to demonstrate deep understanding of the customer’s situation, motivations, and underlying concerns. And get the customer confirmation.

 

Use the following storytelling arc inspired by Ben Zoldan Storyleader approach:

 

Setting: what the company does; what their success means; was there an M&A in play; highlite any proud moment getting where they are now (if applicable); can add how they are using us (if valuable to mention).

 

Complication: the pain; the problem that is encountered; what they are talking to us; This is not a need for a solution, this is a problem; it’s the why of the discussion.

 

Turning point: ah ha moment; what if we were doing this, how that would help; the realization.

 

Resolution: what an end state would look like; what ideal looks like.

 

Finish by asking the customer to confirm if we got this right.

 

Instructions: Write in clear, human language suitable for an email.

Do not list technologies unless they directly support the emotional or business tension.

Do not repeat facts verbatim from the notes.

Translate facts into intent, pressure, frustration, ambition, or risk.

Explicitly surface what feels uncomfortable, risky, or heavy for the customer.

If emotions are not stated directly, infer them cautiously and reasonably (as in “maybe this is causing you…”).

Assume the reader is the customer and ask yourself: “Would they say yes, that’s exactly it?”

 

Tone guidelines:

Curious

Respectful

Empathetic

Strategic

Not salesy

Not technical documentation

 

Length guidelines: 4 short paragraphs (as per story arc described earlier)

No bullet points if possible

No dashes in all cases

No section headers in the final output

End the story with a sentence that invites correction, such as: “Let me know where this resonates or where we missed the mark.”
